text: Today is moving day. We're moving from Seoul to Busan, so I'm going to teach you
some English expressions used while moving, starting from packing and preparing all the way to
enjoying Cha Jung-myun in our new place. We spent the week sorting through our things and packing
all our belongings into boxes. We used bubble wrap to protect anything fragile in labeled
each box so we'd know exactly where everything should go later. We emptied out our storage unit
and since we're trying to live a more minimalist lifestyle, we got rid of as much as we could
by selling things on Dangan. We hired a moving company, so on moving day, the mover helped us
carry our boxes and clear out the apartment. We did one last walk through and set our final
goodbye. He loaded everything into the back of the truck and strapped it down to keep it secure
during the ride. We're in the moving truck now on our way to Busan. It's about a five-hour drive.
It's quite a long trip from Seoul to Busan, so we're taking a quick break and grabbing a bite to eat
at the rest stop. After eating, we'll probably go to the gas station and fill up our tanks before
getting back on the road. You can never go wrong with dumplings. They're very plump
and juicy. Rest stop food in Korea. Never let me down. The driver gave us some ice cold waters.
So necessary today.
Welcome to our new home. We just finished unloading the truck and wow it's so messy now.
We've got our work cut out for us tonight. This apartment is a bit small but it's cozy. It's time
to start unpacking. I hate this part. Our last apartment was fully furnished, so we didn't have any
of our own furniture, but this place was completely empty. It was unfurnished, so we had to buy
a few necessities like this mattress. We need somewhere to sleep tonight. Let's do some unboxing.
When moving, you always need a box cutter. These things come in handy. I'm tipping the box
over. Let's use these muscles. Okay, I got this. I feel like it's going to explode if like the
tent at the Han. Done. Let's test it out. Wow. It looks a little bit sad right now. We don't have
a bed frame yet and it's quite thin because it was vacuum sealed, so it's still expanding. But
it's not bad. It's pretty comfortable. I'm unpacking boxes and getting organized. For such a small
apartment, there's surprisingly a lot of storage, so I'm figuring out where to put everything.
I put our snacks and some food in these cupboards. We have a lot of seaweed. Every time I move,
it takes me about a week before I finally decide where things go. I'm constantly moving things
around and changing where they go. We also ordered some furniture online, so I measured the space
using a measuring tape to make sure everything would fit. And we assembled everything ourselves.
It's time for Korea's moving tradition. Having well-deserved jjajangmyeon and tangsuyuk.
I ordered through kupang eats. I'm so happy to know that it's just as fast here in Busan. I'll try
the tangsuyuk first. Do you pour the sauce onto the tangsuyuk or do you dip the tangsuyuk?
Personally, I'm a dipper. Jingmok. Not bad. It especially hits the spot after a long day of moving.
I pour the sauce on it. What? Did I do this wrong? Oh, because that one's bigger.
Okay, okay. Okay. There we go. We fixed it. I let it sit out for a little bit. So the noodles are a
little bit soggy, but it's okay. I love this tradition. To be honest, I don't think I eat jjajangmyeon
on any other day except for moving day. And I don't know why because I love it. But I guess maybe
it makes it extra special. I only eat it on a special occasion.
My favorite spot in the new apartment is definitely the kitchen. It's the best room here.
It's much larger than my old kitchen. It has a lot more counter space and cupboards for storage.
I'm obsessed. I'll try to do even more cooking lives or cooking videos for you guys. It's been
